1: "Abraham had taken another wife, whose name was Keturah." The remarriage after Sarah's death raises an ANE legal question: what is Keturah's status? 1 Chronicles 1:32 calls her a pîlegeš (concubine), while the Hebrew here uses ʾiššāh (wife/woman). The distinction matters for inheritance: a wife's sons inherit; a concubine's sons receive gifts at best (v6). Abraham's continued fertility into old age (he would have been ~140 at this point if she bore his last son before he died at 175) is sometimes read as a parallel to Isaac's miraculous birth — a sign of ongoing divine vitality, though the covenant heir is already established as Isaac.
2: Keturah's six sons — Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah — are the ancestral eponyms of Arabian/desert peoples. Midian will be the most significant in later biblical history: Moses flees to Midian (Exodus 2:15), marries Zipporah the Midianite, and receives his priestly formation from Jethro. Midianite traders appear in the Joseph story (Genesis 37:28,36). The Midianites later become Israel's enemies in the time of Gideon (Judg 6-8). This verse establishes that Abraham is the ancestor of peoples with whom Israel will have complex relationships across centuries.
3: Sheba and Dedan are the sons of Jokshan; Sheba is likely the Arabian kingdom of the Queen of Sheba who visits Solomon (1 Kings 10:1-13). Dedan is a well-attested Arabian trading city in northwestern Arabia (Ezekiel 25:13; Genesis 27:15,20; Isaiah 21:13). The descendants of Dedan — the Ashurites, Letushites, and Leummites — are otherwise unknown from extrabiblical sources; they were probably nomadic clan-groups whose identities were absorbed into larger tribal formations. The placement of Sheba and Dedan here (as descendants of Keturah through Jokshan) coexists with another Sheba/Dedan genealogy in Genesis 10:7 (descendants of Ham through Cush and Raamah) — reflecting the fluidity of ANE genealogical traditions for shared-territory peoples.
4: "The sons of Midian" — five sub-clans. Ephah appears in Isaiah 60:6 as a Midianite clan bringing gold and incense to Jerusalem. Hanok is the same name as Enoch (Ḥănôk), a shared Semitic personal name. The five sub-clans of Midian structure the genealogy in parallel to the twelve tribal units of Israel: Abraham's non-covenant lines also develop internal genealogical organization. The summary "all these were descendants of Keturah" closes the secondary-wife genealogy and marks these peoples as related to but distinct from the covenant-line — sharing Abrahamic ancestry but not Abrahamic covenant-election.
5: "Abraham left everything he owned to Isaac" — this is the inheritance declaration. Kol ʾăšer-lô (everything he had) is the comprehensive formula. The legal arrangement follows the ANE convention for a primary heir: the firstborn-of-the-primary-wife received the full inheritance. Though Isaac was not Abraham's biological firstborn (Ishmael was born first), YHWH's election of Isaac as covenant-heir (Genesis 17:19-21; Genesis 21:12) and the removal of Ishmael from the household (Genesis 21:14) established Isaac's uncontested legal inheritance position. The formal statement here serves as the testament before the narrative of the secondary-sons' settlement (v6).
6: "He gave gifts to the sons of his concubines and sent them away... to the land of the east." The word here for "concubines" is pîlagšîm (plural of pîlegeš) — the same status 1 Chronicles 1:32 assigns Keturah. The gift-versus-inheritance distinction is legally significant: the Code of Hammurabi (§170-171) addresses the sons of a secondary wife: if acknowledged, they share inheritance; if given gifts and sent away, they forfeit inheritance claims. Abraham's arrangement — gifts plus geographic separation — removes the secondary sons from the inheritance zone and preempts future legal conflict with Isaac. "The land of the east" (ʾereṣ qeḏem) is the general term for Arabia and the Syrian desert, the territory of the pastoral/nomadic peoples descended from these sons.
7: "Abraham lived a hundred and seventy-five years." The patriarchal life-spans in Genesis are decreasing from Adam's 930 (Genesis 5) through Noah's 950 (Genesis 9:29), Shem's 600 (Genesis 11:10), and Abraham's 175 — the trajectory toward normal human life-span. In ANE cultural context, long life was a sign of divine blessing and wisdom; the patriarchs' extreme ages mark them as figures of an earlier, more-blessed era. Abraham's 175 years place his death chronologically when Isaac is 75 and Jacob and Esau are 15 (since Isaac married at 40 and the twins were born 20 years later at v26: Isaac was 60).
8: "Breathed his last and died at a good old age, an old man full of years; and he was gathered to his people." Three parallel phrases mark Abraham's death as complete and honorable: (1) wayyigwaʿ (breathed his last — the same verb for animal death: Numbers 20:29; used for patriarchal deaths in a formulaic sense), (2) śĕḇaʿ yāmîm (full of days/years — the idiom of a sated, complete life: Job 42:17; 1 Chronicles 29:28), and (3) wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw (was gathered to his people). The last phrase implies reunion with deceased ancestors — a concept of post-mortem communal identity that is distinct from later Sheol theology. The gathered-to-people formula presupposes that the "people" to whom one is gathered exist and are accessible, suggesting that Abraham expected a continued existence after death among his ancestors.
9: "His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah." The joint burial of Abraham by the two rival brothers is significant: despite their separation (Genesis 21), both sons return for their father's burial. In ANE culture, burial of a parent was a sacred obligation that superseded family conflict (the Egyptian Instructions of Ani; the Akkadian advice literature). The cave of Machpelah had been purchased by Abraham for Sarah's burial at a precise legal price (400 shekels, Genesis 23) — it was now Abraham's established family tomb. Mamre was his settled home-base in Canaan. The presence of Ishmael at the burial is the last recorded act of the Ishmael narrative; he and Isaac will next appear only in their own genealogical closings.
10: "The field Abraham had bought from the Hittites. There Abraham was buried with his wife Sarah." The double restatement of the Machpelah purchase and the joint burial of Abraham with Sarah is the legal record: the deed of purchase (Genesis 23) established ownership; the burials establish the tomb's identity as the patriarchal family grave. The Machpelah cave at Hebron will become the burial site for Isaac and Rebekah (Genesis 35:27-29) and Leah (Genesis 49:31) as well; Jacob requests and receives burial there (Genesis 49:30; Genesis 50:12-13). The family tomb at Machpelah is the physical anchor of the covenant family's claim to Canaan — a purchased, witnessed, legally undeniable deed in the land of promise.
11: "After Abraham's death, God blessed his son Isaac, who then lived near Beer Lahai Roi." The covenant's transfer to Isaac is stated with brevity — YHWH blessed Isaac — but the single statement is the narrative's most important transition: the Abrahamic covenant now passes to the second generation. "Beer Lahai Roi" (bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî, the well of the Living One who sees me) is the place of Hagar's angel encounter (Genesis 16:14) and where Isaac was living when Rebekah arrived from Paddan-Aram (Genesis 24:62). Isaac's life at Hagar's sacred well creates a subtle narrative link between the two branches of Abraham's family — Isaac lives at the place sacred to Ishmael's mother.
12: "This is the account (toledot) of the family line of Abraham's son Ishmael." The second toledot formula in Genesis 25 (after the Abraham-toledot that opened this chapter in the wider narrative structure). The toledot formula appears ten times in Genesis as a literary structural marker; this is the toledot of Ishmael — a formal closing of one narrative thread before opening the main thread (Isaac's toledot follows in v19). The identification of Ishmael's mother as "Sarah's slave, Hagar the Egyptian" — with both the slave-status and ethnic identity — frames the Ishmael genealogy within the complicated social hierarchy of the previous narrative: Ishmael was born of a slave woman, which determined his inheritance status.
13: "Listed in the order of their birth: Nebaioth the firstborn of Ishmael" — the birth-order listing signals a formal genealogical record. Nebaioth is identified in later sources with the Nabataeans, the Arabian people who built Petra (in modern Jordan) and controlled the incense trade routes. Kedar is the most prominent Ishmaelite tribe in later OT prophecy (Isaiah 21:16-17; Genesis 42:11; Genesis 60:7; Jeremiah 2:10; Genesis 49:28-29; Psalms 120:5) — an Arab pastoral people of the Syrian desert known for their black tents. The Kedarites are the ANE trade and military force in the Arabian world. The twelve sons of Ishmael structurally parallel the twelve sons of Jacob — an intentional literary parallel suggesting that Ishmael's line also has a complete tribal structure, though outside the covenant.
14: Mishma, Dumah, Massa — three more Ishmaelite son-names. Dumah appears in Isaiah 21:11 as an Arabian place-name ("the oracle concerning Dumah" — likely the oasis of Dumat al-Jandal in northwestern Arabia). Massa appears in Proverbs 30:1 and 31:1 as the tribe of the sages Agur and Lemuel — suggesting a wisdom tradition in the Ishmaelite tribal world that intersects with Israelite wisdom literature. The tribal genealogy thus identifies peoples who will appear in later biblical narrative at various points of intersection with Israel.
15: Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah. Tema is an important Arabian oasis city (modern Tayma in northwestern Arabia) mentioned in Isaiah 21:14 and Job 6:19 — a major caravan hub. Jetur and Naphish appear in 1 Chronicles 5:19 as peoples against whom the Reubenites and Gadites fought east of the Jordan — confirming the Ishmaelite tribes' range into Transjordan and northern Arabia. Kedemah means "eastward/eastern" — possibly a geographic-direction eponym for the easternmost Ishmaelite settlements.
16: "Twelve tribal rulers (nĕśîʾîm) according to their settlements and camps." The twelve-ruler structure of Ishmael's line precisely mirrors the twelve tribes of Jacob/Israel. The word nāśîʾ (ruler, prince, one who is lifted up) is the term used for tribal chiefs in Israel's wilderness period (Numbers 1:16; Genesis 7:2). Both "settlements" (ḥaṣerêhem, encampments/villages) and "camps" (ṭirōtām, enclosures/camps) reflect the semi-nomadic lifestyle of the Ishmaelite tribes — moving between settled locations and nomadic camping grounds seasonally. The parallel twelve-unit structure is a structural statement: YHWH's promise to Ishmael (Genesis 17:20: "I will make him into a great nation") has been fulfilled with the same formal organization as the covenant people.
17: "Ishmael lived a hundred and thirty-seven years. He breathed his last and died, and he was gathered to his people." Ishmael's death notice uses the same wayyigwaʿ (breathed his last) and wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw (gathered to his people) formulas as Abraham's death (v8). The parallel formula is not accidental: Ishmael, though outside the covenant-election, receives an honorable death notice in the same formal language as his father. His 137 years place him within the decreasing life-span trajectory of the post-Flood patriarchs, shorter than his father Abraham's 175 but longer than his half-brother Isaac's 180 years (Genesis 35:28).
18: "From Havilah to Shur, near the eastern border of Egypt." The geographic range describes the Arabian Peninsula and Sinai region. Havilah appears in Genesis 2:11 as a land of gold; here it marks the eastern edge of the Ishmaelite territorial range. Shur is in the Sinai, the desert wilderness between Egypt and Canaan (where Hagar fled in Genesis 16:7). "As you go toward Ashur" (ʿal-pĕnê ʾaššûr, before/in front of Assyria) — the directional landmark locates the territory relative to Assyria to the northeast. "Lived in hostility toward all his brothers" — the word nāpal (fell) is sometimes rendered "settled" (the NASB) but the idiom ʿal-pĕnê (over against / in the face of) combined with ʾaḥāyw (his brothers) echoes Genesis 16:12's oracle: Ishmael will "live over against all his brothers" — fulfilling the angel's word to Hagar.
19: "This is the account (toledot) of the family line of Abraham's son Isaac." The ninth toledot formula in Genesis introduces the main narrative thread that will run through the end of the book. The repetition "Abraham was the father of Isaac" after the Ishmael-toledot (which closed Ishmael's line) formally hands the narrative torch from the Ishmael-thread to the Isaac-thread. The double identification — "Abraham's son Isaac" and "Abraham became the father of Isaac" — emphasizes the covenant's continuity through the specific father-son chain YHWH established.
20: "Isaac was forty years old when he married Rebekah daughter of Bethuel the Aramean from Paddan Aram." The marriage at 40 and the 20-year gap before the twins' birth (Isaac was 60 at their birth, v26) structures the barrenness theme. "Paddan Aram" (paddān ʾărām, the plain of Aram) is the region around Harran in northern Mesopotamia (modern southeastern Turkey/northern Syria) — the same region where Laban lived (Genesis 28:2-7) and where Jacob will spend 20 years (29-31). The twice-repeated "the Aramean" for both Bethuel and Laban establishes the Aramean identity of the covenant-family's maternal line — important because Jacob's sons will also be half-Aramean, and the confession "my father was a wandering Aramean" (Deuteronomy 26:5) will summarize the covenant-family's ethnic origins.
21: "Isaac prayed to the LORD on behalf of his wife, because she was childless." Rebekah's barrenness follows the same pattern as Sarah's (Genesis 11:30) — the covenant heir's birth is a divine gift, not human biological productivity. The verb wayyeʿtar (he prayed/entreated) may derive from a root meaning "to burn incense" (offering a petitionary prayer at an altar) or simply "to pray urgently." The twenty years of barrenness before YHWH "answered" (wayyēʿāter, the same verb root in passive/reflexive) show that Isaac's prayer was sustained over a long period, not a quick request. The contrast with Abraham and Sarah's situation: Abraham's solution was to use Hagar (Genesis 16); Isaac prays and waits, a more direct covenant-trust model.
22: "The babies jostled each other within her." The word wayyitroṣeṣû (they were struggling/crushing) describes intense fetal movement — more than normal; the intensity prompted Rebekah's theological question. "She went to inquire of the LORD" (liḏrōš ʾet-YHWH) — the technical term dāraš (to seek, inquire) is the vocabulary of oracle-seeking at a sanctuary. This is the first use of this technical formula for a woman seeking a divine oracle directly. Where she went is not specified — perhaps a local altar, a sacred site, or through a prophetic intermediary. Her question "Why is this happening to me?" (lāmmāh zeh ʾānōkî, "why do I exist?" — literally "why is this, I?") expresses existential bewilderment at the painful pregnancy.
23: "Two nations are in your womb, and two peoples from within you will be separated." The oracle is structured in four parallel lines: (1) two nations (gôyim), (2) two peoples (leʾummîm), (3) one stronger than the other, (4) the older serving the younger. The word leʾummîm (peoples, communities) is a poetic word for ethnic groups. The oracle does not name Jacob or Esau specifically — it addresses Rebekah as the mother of these two groups. The "older serving the younger" reversal anticipates the repeated younger-over-older pattern in Genesis (Isaac over Ishmael; Jacob over Esau; Ephraim over Manasseh) and gives it divine sanction from the womb. Paul cites this oracle in Romans 9:10-13 as a demonstration of divine election operating before birth and independent of human action.
24: "There were twin boys in her womb." The twin birth in the OT is significant: twins appear in Genesis 38:27-30 (Perez and Zerah, Judah's sons by Tamar), and they will be important for the Davidic line (Matthew 1:3). Twins create built-in ambiguity about birth-order precedence: which one is "firstborn" when both were conceived together? The womb-struggle (v22) already signaled the precedence-competition. In ANE law (various Mesopotamian legal texts), the firstborn who emerged first was the legal heir, regardless of which twin was conceived first — the narrative's careful recording of which came out first is therefore the legal question of inheritance rights.
25: "The first to come out was red, and his whole body was like a hairy garment; so they named him Esau." The Hebrew wordplay: ʾadmônî (red/ruddy) anticipates ʾĕḏôm (Edom, the nation of Esau's descendants); the "hairy garment" (kĕʾadderaṯ śēʿār) connects to śĕʿîr (Seir, the mountain territory of Edom) and to ʿēśāw (Esau, perhaps related to the word for "hairy"). The triple wordplay — red/Edom, hairy/Seir, Esau — is built into the birth narrative: the man and his nation are described in a single pun. This kind of etiological name-play is standard in Genesis (Adam/ʾăḏāmāh, Eve/ḥāyāh, Cain/qānāh, Abel/heḇel, etc.), but Esau's naming packs three geographic/ethnic identifications into one description.
26: "His brother came out, with his hand grasping Esau's heel; so he was named Jacob." The heel-grasping at birth is the etiological explanation for the name yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob) — from ʿāqēḇ (heel) via the verb ʿāqaḇ (to grab the heel, and by extension to supplant/deceive). Hosea 12:3 cites the heel-grasping: "in the womb he grasped his brother's heel." The same verb ʿāqaḇ appears in Esau's accusation in Genesis 27:36: "He has deceived me (wayyaʿqĕḇēnî) these two times — he took my birthright, and now he's taken my blessing." The heel-grabbing at birth is thus a character-signature: Jacob's entire life is a working-out of this birth-instinct. "Isaac was sixty years old when Rebekah gave birth" — the 20-year gap from marriage (v20) to birth confirms the extended barrenness.
27: "Esau became a skillful hunter, a man of the open country, while Jacob was content to stay at home among the tents." The contrast is sharp in ANE social terms: the hunter (ṣayāḏ) lives in wild, uncultivated territory (śāḏeh, open field/country), while the tent-dweller (yōšēḇ ʾohālîm, one who sits/dwells in tents) represents the settled pastoral economy. Hunters in the ANE were associated with the pre-agricultural, wild-territory culture; tent-dwellers were the semi-settled pastoralists with flocks and herds. Nimrod is described as a "mighty hunter" (Genesis 10:9) with a connotation of warrior-prowess; Esau's hunter identity connects him to this pre-covenant figure. The description of Jacob as tām (complete, blameless, simple — the same word used of Noah in 6:9 and Job in Job 1:1) qualifies his character before the deception narratives complicate it.
28: "Isaac, who had a taste for wild game, loved Esau, but Rebekah loved Jacob." The parental favoritism is stated with candid directness. Isaac's love for Esau is given a physical reason (kî-ṣayiḏ bĕpîw, because game was in his mouth — his preference for Esau's wild game), while Rebekah's love for Jacob is stated without reason. The conditional character of Isaac's stated reason — his love depends on what Esau provides — is a subtle narrative commentary. In ANE patriarchal families, parental favoritism was not unusual, but the covenantal consequences of Isaac's preference for Esau (who despises his birthright) and Rebekah's for Jacob (who will carry the covenant) shape the entire deception narrative of Genesis 27.
29: "Once when Jacob was cooking some stew, Esau came in from the open country, famished." The scene is a classic ANE contrasting-brothers encounter: one working at domestic provision (cooking), the other returning empty from the hunt. The word ʿāyēp (famished/exhausted/weary) is the same word used for David's exhausted troops in 2 Samuel 16:2; it implies genuine physical depletion but not mortal danger. The lentil stew (nĕzîḏ ʿădāšîm, cooked lentil dish) — lentils were a common staple food of the ancient Levant, the food of the poor and middle classes. The scene's irony is that the great hunter returns from the field unable to feed himself, while the tent-dweller has a pot of food ready.
30: "Quick, let me have some of that red stew! I'm famished!" The Hebrew is vivid and colloquial: halʿîṭēnî nāʾ min-hāʾāḏōm hāʾāḏōm hazzeh (feed me, please, from that red, red [thing]) — the repetition of ʾāḏōm (red) twice, with the article hazzeh (this one), is emphatic and almost breathless. Esau cannot even name what he wants — he calls it "the red stuff" — a detail that highlights his fixation on immediate appetite over consideration. "That is why he was also called Edom" — the etiological comment: Edom (ʾĕḏôm, the nation of Esau's descendants) derives its name from Esau's craving for the red food. The Edomites as a people are named after their ancestor's moment of appetite-driven loss.
31: "First sell me your birthright." Jacob's counter-demand is not spontaneous exploitation — it is a deliberate negotiation. The birthright (bĕḵōrāh) was the firstborn's legal right to a double portion of the inheritance (Deuteronomy 21:17) plus the family headship and, in this covenant family, the covenant-heir status. In ANE law (Nuzi texts from the 15th-14th century BCE document similar transactions among Hurrians), birthrights could be sold or transferred — they were legally tradeable property rights, not merely social customs. Jacob's "first" (kāʿēt — "sell me first" or "sell me right now") creates urgency: he is timing the offer to Esau's moment of maximum desperation.
32: "Look, I am about to die. What good is the birthright to me?" Esau's argument is hyperbole in an ANE context: hunters knew hunger well; this one meal was not going to cause death. The word hōlēk lāmût (I am going to die) is the present participle of dying — "I am about to die," not "I am dying right now." The dismissal of the birthright as worthless under present circumstances is the narrative's key theological statement: Esau cannot see beyond his immediate physical need to the long-term covenantal value of what he holds. The author of Hebrews (Genesis 12:16) will call Esau "godless" (bébēlos) for this exchange — not because he was immoral by other standards but because he treated the sacred (covenant-heir status) as profane (exchangeable for food).
33: "But Jacob said, 'Swear to me first.' So he swore an oath to him, selling his birthright to Jacob." The sworn oath (wĕyiššāḇaʿ lô) is the legal formalization of the transaction. Oral contracts in the ANE were binding when accompanied by an oath sworn in the name of a deity. Jacob's insistence on the sworn oath converts what might have been a jesting exchange into a legally enforceable transfer. Nuzi texts from the ancient Near East (15th-century Hurrian legal documents) record actual cases where birthrights were traded for food and goods — the practice was apparently known and legally valid, though socially unusual. The oath-taking in Genesis is always serious: Abraham's servant swears (Genesis 24:9), Jacob swears to Laban (Genesis 31:53), Esau swears here.
34: "Then Jacob gave Esau some bread and some lentil stew. He ate and drank, and then got up and left. So Esau despised his birthright." The narrator's five rapid verbs — ate, drank, got up, left, despised — deliver the verdict efficiently: the transaction was consummated with a meal (a form of covenant-ratification in ANE culture: eating together sealed agreements), and then Esau simply left. The final statement "So Esau despised (wayyiḇez ʿēśāw) his birthright" is the narrator's theological verdict — the verb bāzāh means to treat as contemptible/worthless (used for despising YHWH's word in 2 Samuel 12:9-10; Malachi 1:6). The birthright was not taken from Esau — he sold it willingly for a meal. The subsequent narrative (Genesis 27) adds the stolen blessing, but the birthright was voluntarily transferred here.
